questions.question: Under which main topic section are Quality of Life Outcomes addressed in the EAU Guidelines on Prostate Cancer?

options.text: Quality of Life Outcomes in Prostate Cancer

questions.explanation: The EAU Guidelines structure explicitly lists 'Quality of Life Outcomes In Prostate Cancer' as a standalone section in the guideline table of contents.

questions.question: Which of the following sections is listed as a separate topic in the EAU Guidelines on Prostate Cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline text lists 'Epidemiology And Aetiology' as a separate section. The other options (Surgical Techniques, Radiation Dosing, Laboratory Standards) are not mentioned in the provided text.

questions.question: What is the time threshold used to define 'long-term consequences' in Section 8.2 of the guideline chapter?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Section 8.2 summarises long-term consequences (≥ twelve months) of therapies for PCa.
questions.source_text: This chapter is presented in two parts. The first part of this chapter (Section 8.2) summarises long-term consequences (≥ twelve months) of therapies for PCa. Based on two SRs. The second part of this chapter (Section 8.3) provides evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients when selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions aimed at improving disease-specific QoL across all stages of disease.

questions.question: What type of recommendations does Section 8.3 provide according to the guideline text?

options.text: Evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients in selecting treatment options

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Section 8.3 provides evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients when selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions aimed at improving disease-specific QoL across all stages of disease.
questions.source_text: This chapter is presented in two parts. The first part of this chapter (Section 8.2) summarises long-term consequences (≥ twelve months) of therapies for PCa. Based on two SRs. The second part of this chapter (Section 8.3) provides evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients when selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions aimed at improving disease-specific QoL across all stages of disease.

questions.question: According to the guideline text, which of the following is specifically mentioned as part of the multidisciplinary team for prostate cancer care?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that a multidisciplinary team includes 'urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, oncology nurses, behavioural practitioners and many others, including fellow patients.' Fellow patients are specifically mentioned as part of the team.
questions.source_text: Quality of life and personalised care go hand in hand. Treating PCa can affect an individual both physically and mentally, as well as close relations and work or vocation. These multifaceted issues all have a bearing on an individual‘s perception of QoL [1489,1490]. Approaching care from a holistic perspective requires the intervention of a multidisciplinary team including urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, oncology nurses, behavioural practitioners and many others, includin

questions.question: According to the guideline text, which subgroup is specifically identified as being at increased risk of long-term mental health issues following prostate cancer treatment?

options.text: Patients with family history of cancer

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Subgroups of people, including those with poor general health, being unmarried, older age and/or pre-existing depressive symptoms, are more at risk of long-term mental health issues following treatment for PCa.' Pre-existing depressive symptoms is directly mentioned as a risk factor.
questions.source_text: Quality of life and personalised care go hand in hand. Treating PCa can affect an individual both physically and mentally, as well as close relations and work or vocation. questions.question: According to the systematic review on long-term health-related quality of life in patients on active surveillance, what is the key finding regarding continence outcomes compared to active treatment?

options.text: Continence is considerably superior in active surveillance compared to radical prostatectomy even after extended periods
Continence outcomes are equivalent between active surveillance and radical prostatectomy after five years
Active surveillance leads to worse continence outcomes compared to radiotherapy
There is no significant difference in continence between any treatment modalities

questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that 'in preservation of continence, there is a clear advantage for AS over active treatment, particularly to RP' and that 'even after extended periods, continence is still considerably superior in AS to that in RP.'
questions.source_text: In an SR [1496] on the long-term (> 5 year) health-related QoL in patients on active surveillance, researchers observed that there were differences in specific functional outcomes between patients on AS and surgery or radiotherapy ≥ five years after treatment. In patients on AS, the overall HRQoL and psychological well-being outcomes were good. All studies comparing AS with active treatment found no substantial or consistent difference in general HRQoL PROMs between groups. In

questions.question: What does the registry on active surveillance in the USA report regarding cancer-specific anxiety in men undergoing active surveillance?

options.text: Anxiety remains high and constant throughout the surveillance period
Anxiety significantly decreases over time, with most men reporting low levels within two years
Anxiety only affects patients with larger tumor volumes
Anxiety is unrelated to the duration of active surveillance

questions.explanation: The text clearly states that 'men undergoing active surveillance had a moderate risk of cancer-specific anxiety that significantly decreases over time' and 'most men rapidly adjust and report low levels of anxiety within two years.'
questions.source_text: In an SR [1496] on the long-term (> 5 year) health-related QoL in patients on active surveillance, researchers observed that there were differences in specific functional outcomes between patients on AS and surgery or radiotherapy ≥ five years after treatment. In patients on AS, the overall HRQoL and psychological well-being outcomes were good. All studies comparing AS with active treatment found no substantial or consistent difference in general HRQoL PROMs between groups. In

questions.question: According to the comparative data between RALP and RRP, what were the continence rates at twelve months in the prospective controlled non-randomised trial?
questions.explanation: The text states that the prospective trial demonstrated at twelve months 21.3% were incontinent after RALP and 20.2% after RRP. The continence rates of 89-100% and 80-97% refer to mean continence rates from systematic reviews, not from the prospective trial. The ED rates of 70.4% and 74.7% are for erectile dysfunction, not continence.
questions.source_text: A lack of clear consensus in reporting surgical complications following RP - specifically urinary incontinence and stricture rates - and the introduction of different techniques has resulted in a wide variation in the types of complications reported, as well as variation in the overall incidence of complications [1498-1501]. The most common postoperative complication is ED, but other related issues to consider include dry ejaculation, which occurs with removal of the prostate, change in the qual

questions.question: What is reported as the most common postoperative complication following radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'The most common postoperative complication is ED' (erectile dysfunction). While long-term incontinence is described as the second most commonly occurring complication, and inguinal hernia and bladder neck contracture are mentioned as related issues, erectile dysfunction is identified as the most common.
questions.source_text: A lack of clear consensus in reporting surgical complications following RP - specifically urinary incontinence and stricture rates - and the introduction of different techniques has resulted in a wide variation in the types of complications reported, as well as variation in the overall incidence of complications [1498-1501]. questions.question: According to the ProtecT trial data, how does IMRT compare to 3D-CRT in terms of bowel toxicity?

options.text: IMRT has similar bowel toxicity to 3D-CRT
IMRT causes more bowel toxicity than 3D-CRT
IMRT demonstrates less bowel toxicity than 3D-CRT
IMRT has been replaced by 3D-CRT in modern practice

questions.explanation: The text states that 'studies using IMRT demonstrate less bowel toxicity than noted previously with 3D-CRT'. The ProtecT trial participants received 3D-CRT, but newer studies with IMRT show improved toxicity profiles.
questions.section_title: 8.2.3.a. Side effects of external beam radiotherapy
questions.source_text: Analysis of the toxicity outcomes of the ProtecT trial shows that patients treated with EBRT and six months of ADT report bowel toxicity including persistent diarrhoea, bowel urgency and/or incontinence and rectal bleeding (as described in detail in Section 8.3.1.a) [1510]. Participants in the ProtecT study were treated with 3D-CRT and studies using IMRT demonstrate less bowel toxicity than noted previously with 3D-CRT [1511]. An SR and meta-analysis of observational studies comparing patients e

questions.question: What is the timeframe for recovery from the temporary drop in EPIC hormonal and sexual domains when six months of ADT is added to radiotherapy?

options.text: At one year, any clinically relevant difference disappears
Patients never recover these functions fully
Recovery occurs after two years of follow-up

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'patient-reported outcomes suggest a temporary drop in the EPIC hormonal and sexual domains when six months of ADT was added to radiotherapy, with a disappearance of any clinical relevant difference at one year.'
questions.section_title: 8.2.3.a. Side effects of external beam radiotherapy

questions.question: What percentage of urinary complications following brachytherapy implantation are caused by urethral strictures?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'urethral strictures account for at least 50% of urinary complications'.
questions.source_text: Some patients experience significant urinary complications following implantation such as urinary retention (1.5-22%) - with post-implantation TURP reported as being required in up to 8.7% of cases - and incontinence (0–19%) [1514]. Chronic urinary morbidity is more common with combined EBRT and BT and can occur in up to 20% of patients, depending on the severity of the symptoms before BT. Urethral strictures account for at least 50% of urinary complications and can be resolved with dilation in

questions.question: Chronic urinary morbidity can occur in up to what percentage of patients when combining EBRT and BT?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'chronic urinary morbidity is more common with combined EBRT and BT and can occur in up to 20% of patients'.
questions.source_text: Some patients experience significant urinary complications following implantation such as urinary retention (1.5-22%) - with post-implantation TURP reported as being required in up to 8.7% of cases - and incontinence (0–19%) [1514]. questions.question: According to the guideline text regarding second cancer risk following external beam radiotherapy, which anatomical site has the highest odds ratio (OR)?
questions.explanation: The text states an increased risk of developing second cancers for bladder (OR: 1.39), colorectal (OR: 1.68) and rectum (OR: 1.62). Colorectal has the highest OR at 1.68.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a side effect specifically from brachytherapy according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The text describes urinary complications from brachytherapy including urinary retention, incontinence, and urethral strictures. Bowel toxicity and rectal bleeding are mentioned as side effects of EBRT (ProtecT trial with 3D-CRT), not brachytherapy specifically.

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis, what was found regarding urinary incontinence at one year when comparing whole-gland cryotherapy to radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: Cryotherapy had significantly higher incontinence rates than RP
Cryotherapy had significantly lower incontinence rates than RP, but the difference decreased with longer follow-up
Cryotherapy had identical incontinence rates to RP at one year
Cryotherapy had no significant difference compared to RP in terms of incontinence

questions.explanation: The text states that 'the rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, but the size of the difference decreased with longer follow-up' [853].
questions.source_text: An SR and meta-analysis produced evidence that the rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, but the size of the difference decreased with longer follow-up [853]. No significant difference was seen between cryotherapy versus EBRT in terms of urinary incontinence at one year (< 1%); cryotherapy had a similar ED rate (range 0–40%) to RP at one year. Whole-gland HIFU on the other hand showed lower incontinence rates at one year than RP (OR:

questions.question: Which treatment modality demonstrated significantly lower incontinence rates compared to radical prostatectomy at one year according to the meta-analysis?

options.text: All of the above showed similar incontinence rates to RP

questions.explanation: The text specifically states that 'Whole-gland HIFU on the other hand showed lower incontinence rates at one year than RP (OR: 0.06, 95% CI: 0.01–0.48)' [853]. Cryotherapy was also lower but not as significantly as HIFU, and EBRT showed no significant difference.
questions.source_text: An SR and meta-analysis produced evidence that the rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, but the size of the difference decreased with longer follow-up [853]. questions.question: According to the systematic review on focal therapy for prostate cancer, what is the pad-free continence rate following focal HIFU and cryotherapy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that for focal HIFU and cryotherapy, pad-free continence rates were above 95%.
questions.source_text: Over the past decade, prostate cancer has been detected at an earlier stage, with smaller tumours and with more patients potentially suitable for focal therapy [858-860]. Focal therapy is seeking the optimal balance regarding cancer control and functional outcome. A recent SR included data from 5,827 patients across 72 studies covering various energy sources and found evidence that focal therapy has favourable functional outcomes and minimises adverse events [864]. For focal HIFU and cryotherapy

questions.question: According to the systematic review comparing focal ablation to whole-gland ablation at one year, what was the reduction in sexual function scores for focal ablation?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that focal ablation showed only 9% reduction in sexual function scores, compared to 43% for whole-gland ablation at one year.

questions.question: According to the systematic review covering 5,827 patients across 72 studies, what was the pad-free continence rate for focal HIFU and cryotherapy?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'for focal HIFU and cryotherapy, this SR showed pad-free continence rates above 95%'.

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'cryotherapy had a similar ED rate (range 0–40%) to RP at one year.'

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of people undergoing androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) experience cessation of sexual activity?
questions.explanation: The text states that cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93%.
questions.source_text: Cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93% [1531]. Androgen-deprivation therapy reduces both libido and the ability to gain and maintain erections. The management of acquired ED is mostly non-specific [1532]. Using a specific nonvalidated questionnaire, bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months [1533]. A post-hoc analysis

questions.question: In a study using a specific nonvalidated questionnaire at twelve months, bicalutamide monotherapy showed significant advantage over castration in which domains?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months.
questions.source_text: Cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93% [1531]. Androgen-deprivation therapy reduces both libido and the ability to gain and maintain erections. The management of acquired ED is mostly non-specific [1532]. Using a specific nonvalidated questionnaire, bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months [1533]. questions.question: According to the guideline text, which statement about hot flushes during ADT is correct?

options.text: They affect approximately 20-30% of men on ADT
They usually appear about 6 months after starting ADT
They have a significant impact on quality of life

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT with prevalence estimated between 44-80%, appear three months after starting ADT (not 6 months), persist long-term, and have a significant impact on QoL.
questions.source_text: Hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT (prevalence estimated between 44–80% of men on ADT) [1531]. They appear three months after starting ADT, usually persist long-term and have a significant impact on QoL. Serotonin re-uptake inhibitors (e.g. venlafaxine or sertraline) appear to be less effective in reducing hot flushes than hormonal therapies based on a prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine, 75 mg daily, with medroxyprogesterone, 20 mg daily, or cyproterone acetate, 100 mg daily [1536].

questions.question: Based on the prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine, medroxyprogesterone, and cyproterone acetate for hot flushes, which statement is supported by the data?

options.text: Venlafaxine showed the best efficacy in reducing hot flushes
Cyproterone acetate demonstrated superior efficacy compared to venlafaxine
All three treatments showed equivalent efficacy

questions.explanation: The study showed median daily hot-flush score reductions of -47.2% for venlafaxine, -94.5% for cyproterone acetate, and -83.7% for medroxyprogesterone, clearly demonstrating that cyproterone acetate was superior to venlafaxine.
questions.source_text: Hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT (prevalence estimated between 44–80% of men on ADT) [1531]. They appear three months after starting ADT, usually persist long-term and have a significant impact on QoL. Serotonin re-uptake inhibitors (e.g. venlafaxine or sertraline) appear to be less effective in reducing hot flushes than hormonal therapies based on a prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine, 75 mg daily, with medroxyprogesterone, 20 mg daily, or cyproterone acetate, 100 mg daily [1536].

questions.question: According to the guideline, what was the incidence of non-metastatic fractures in patients receiving enzalutamide, apalutamide, or darolutamide in combination with ADT?
questions.explanation: The text states that the incidence of non-metastatic fractures was 242 (4%) in the enzalutamide/apalutamide/darolutamide group compared to 107 (2%) in the control group.
questions.source_text: ADT leads to increased bone turnover and decreased bone mineral density (BMD) over time, which significantly elevates the risk of fractures. The relative risk of fractures increases by up to 45% with long-term ADT [1541]. Severe fractures in men are associated with increased mortality [1542], underscoring the importance of early identification of high-risk patients and timely preventive measures. A precise evaluation of BMD should be performed by DEXA, ideally before starting long-term ADT. A lo

questions.question: Which of the following is listed as a risk factor for fractures in men receiving ADT?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly lists 'BMI of 19 or less' as one of the risk factors for fractures. Other listed risk factors include increasing age, history of previous fracture or parent with fractured hip, current smoking, glucocorticoid use, rheumatoid arthritis, alcohol consumption > 2 units/day, history of falls, and other chronic medical conditions.
questions.source_text: ADT leads to increased bone turnover and decreased bone mineral density (BMD) over time, which significantly elevates the risk of fractures. questions.question: According to the guideline, what happens to insulin sensitivity during ADT?

options.text: Insulin sensitivity increases with elevated fasting plasma insulin levels
Insulin sensitivity is improved with compensatory hyperinsulinemia
Insulin sensitivity decreases with increased fasting plasma insulin levels
Insulin sensitivity remains unchanged throughout treatment

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that ADT decreases insulin sensitivity and increases fasting plasma insulin levels, which is a marker of insulin resistance.
questions.source_text: Lipid alterations are common and may occur as early as the first three months of ADT [1544]. In addition, ADT also decreases insulin sensitivity and increases fasting plasma insulin levels, which is a marker of insulin resistance. In diabetic patients, metformin appears to be an attractive option for protection against metabolic effects based on retrospective analysis [1551], but there is insufficient data to recommend its use in non-diabetic patients. Metabolic syndrome is an association of ind

questions.question: What percentage of lean body mass decline was reported at three years in men on ADT?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the prospective longitudinal study reported a progressive decline in lean body mass of 2.4% at three years.
questions.source_text: Lipid alterations are common and may occur as early as the first three months of ADT [1544]. In addition, ADT also decreases insulin sensitivity and increases fasting plasma insulin levels, which is a marker of insulin resistance. questions.question: According to the guideline text, what did the phase III RCT comparing relugolix with leuprolide demonstrate?

options.text: Leuprolide was associated with fewer major adverse cardiovascular events than relugolix
Relugolix demonstrated a significantly reduced risk of major adverse cardiovascular events compared to leuprolide
Both drugs showed equivalent cardiovascular safety profiles over 48 weeks
Relugolix increased the risk of cardiovascular events by 46% compared to leuprolide

questions.explanation: The text states that relugolix, an oral LHRH antagonist, was associated with a reduced risk of major adverse cardiovascular events compared to leuprolide (2.9% vs 6.2%) with HR 0.46 (95% CI: 0.24-0.88) over 48 weeks.
questions.source_text: Cardiovascular mortality is a common cause of death in PCa patients [1143,1557,1558]. Several studies showed that even short-term ADT (as early as six months) was associated with an increased risk of diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, and myocardial infarction [1559]. The RTOG 92-02 [1560] and 94-08 [1561] trials confirmed an increased cardiovascular risk, unrelated to the duration of ADT and not accompanied by an overall increased cardiovascular mortality. This was confirmed in the 20-y

questions.question: What finding was reported in the 20-year update of the NRG/RTOG 9202 trial regarding cardiovascular outcomes?

options.text: Long-term ADT significantly increased overall cardiovascular mortality compared to short-term ADT
No difference in cardiovascular outcomes was observed between long-term and short-term ADT
A significantly higher rate of fatal myocardial infarctions was found in men with baseline cardiovascular disease
ADT duration was the strongest predictor of cardiovascular events regardless of patient history

questions.explanation: The text states that the 20-year update found no overall increase in CVM with long-term versus short-term ADT, but reported a significantly higher rate of fatal myocardial infarctions in men with baseline CVD.
questions.source_text: Cardiovascular mortality is a common cause of death in PCa patients [1143,1557,1558]. Several studies showed that even short-term ADT (as early as six months) was associated with an increased risk of diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, and myocardial infarction [1559]. The RTOG 92-02 [1560] and 94-08 [1561] trials confirmed an increased cardiovascular risk, unrelated to the duration of ADT and not accompanied by an overall increased cardiovascular mortality. This was confirmed in the 20-y

questions.question: What is the best protective measure for fatigue developing as a side effect of ADT (Androgen Deprivation Therapy)?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure' for fatigue related to ADT. Iron supplementation, blood transfusion, and vitamin B12 injection are not mentioned as protective measures for ADT-related fatigue.
questions.source_text: Fatigue often develops as a side effect of ADT, for which regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure. Reporting clinically significant fatigue is associated with severe psychological distress and should prompt screening for anxiety and/or depression [1578]. Anaemia may be a cause of fatigue [1531,1579]. If present, anaemia requires further evaluation to identify the cause (medullar invasion, renal insufficiency, iron deficiency, chronic bleeding) and treatment should be individua

questions.question: When a patient reports clinically significant fatigue during ADT, what should be prompted according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The text directly states that 'reporting clinically significant fatigue is associated with severe psychological distress and should prompt screening for anxiety and/or depression.' Testosterone supplementation, discontinuation of ADT, and bone density screening are not mentioned as responses to clinically significant fatigue.
questions.source_text: Fatigue often develops as a side effect of ADT, for which regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure. Reporting clinically significant fatigue is associated with severe psychological distress and should prompt screening for anxiety and/or depression [1578]. questions.question: According to the guideline, castration is associated with an increased risk of which cardiovascular complication?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that castration is associated with an increased risk of stroke.
questions.source_text: Castration also appears to be associated with an increased risk of stroke [1580], and is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline such as Alzheimer disease [1581].

questions.question: According to the guideline, castration is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for which of the following?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that castration is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline such as Alzheimer disease.
questions.source_text: Castration also appears to be associated with an increased risk of stroke [1580], and is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline such as Alzheimer disease [1581].

questions.question: According to the guideline text, what was found regarding cognitive effects of ADT in a prospective observational study with three-year follow-up?

options.text: Significant cognitive decline was observed in men on ADT compared to healthy controls
ADT was associated with moderate cognitive impairment requiring intervention
No association with cognitive decline was demonstrated in men on ADT compared to PCa patients not treated with ADT and healthy controls
Cognitive decline was only observed in patients with metastatic disease

questions.explanation: The text states: 'A prospective observational study with follow-up out to three years failed to demonstrate any association with cognitive decline in men on ADT when compared to men with PCa not treated with ADT and healthy controls.' This directly matches option C.
questions.source_text: Quality of life Androgen-deprivation therapy impacts sexual function, mood, depression, cognitive function, as well as the relationship with the patient’s partner [1515,1516]. A meta-analysis of over 7,500 patients confirmed that ADT significantly impairs QoL, particularly emotional, physical, and cognitive functioning, with greater declines seen after six months of treatment [1517]. A small RCT evaluated the QoL at one-year follow-up in patients with PSA-only relapse after primary therapy witho

questions.question: What does the pooled analysis from the MARCAP Consortium suggest regarding the optimal effective castration period based on testosterone recovery?

options.text: 3-4 months is optimal for testosterone recovery
12-15 months provides the best oncologic outcomes
The optimal effective castration period including treatment duration and time to testosterone recovery was approximately 10-11 months
No specific duration was identified as optimal

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'While testosterone recovery was not linearly associated with MFS, a non-linear benefit was seen with six-month ADT, suggesting that the optimal effective castration period (including treatment duration and the time to testosterone recovery) was approximately 10-11 months in this analysis.' This matches option C exactly.
questions.source_text: Quality of life Androgen-deprivation therapy impacts sexual function, mood, depression, cognitive function, as well as the relationship with the patient’s partner [1515,1516]. A meta-analysis of over 7,500 patients confirmed that ADT significantly impairs QoL, particularly emotional, physical, and cognitive functioning, with greater declines seen after six months of treatment [1517]. A small RCT evaluated the QoL at one-year follow-up in patients with PSA-only relapse after primary therapy witho

questions.question: What is the specific mechanism of action of denosumab?

options.text: Inhibition of receptor activator of nuclear factor-kappa B ligand (RANKL)
Inhibition of pyrophosphate breakdown in bone matrix
Direct binding to hydroxyapatite crystals in bone

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that denosumab acts by 'inhibiting receptor activator of nuclear factor-kappa B ligand (RANKL)'.
questions.section_title: 8.3. Overall quality of life in men with PCa
questions.source_text: Bisphosphonates are synthetic pyrophosphate analogues used in the treatment of conditions such as bone malignancy and osteoporosis. Infrequent side effects associated with bisphosphonate use include pyrexia, renal function impairment, hypocalcemia and avascular osteonecrosis of the jaw. Denosumab is a human monoclonal antibody that is used in the treatment of osteoporosis and bone metastasis [1582,1583]. It acts by inhibiting osteoclast activity, reducing bone resorption, and increasing bone den

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the incidence of osteonecrosis of the jaw after 12 months of bisphosphonate therapy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'the risk ranges from greater than 1% at twelve months to 11% after four years of treatment'.

questions.question: Which combination of PROMs has the best evidence for psychometric properties and feasibility for use in patients with localised prostate cancer?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'the tools with the best evidence for psychometric properties and feasibility for use in routine practice and research settings to assess PROMs in patients with localised PCa were EORTC QLQ-C30 and QLQ-PR25'.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1. Long-term (> twelve months) quality of life outcomes in men with localised disease
questions.source_text: Living longer with PCa does not necessarily equate to living well [1489,1491]. There is clear evidence of unmet needs and ongoing support requirements for some individuals and partners after diagnosis and treatment for PCa [1587,1588]. Fear of cancer recurrence and PSA anxiety has a prevalence of 16% and 22%, respectively, across studies [1589]. Combined cognitive- and education-based psychological interventions improve depression, anxiety and distress [1590]. Cancer impacts on the wider family

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the prevalence of PSA anxiety across studies in prostate cancer patients?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'Fear of cancer recurrence and PSA anxiety has a prevalence of 16% and 22%, respectively, across studies', making 22% the correct answer for PSA anxiety.

questions.question: According to the ProtecT trial results at six years, which treatment group had the highest rate of erectile dysfunction?
questions.explanation: The text states that after RP, 95% reported ED persisting for 85% at six years, while after EBRT this was 69% and 74%, respectively. No data on brachytherapy ED rates is provided in this excerpt.
questions.source_text: In the updated results of the ProtecT trial [1607], treatment-received analyses revealed different impacts of treatments over six years. Men remaining on AM experienced gradual declines in sexual and urinary function with age with increases in ED from 35% at baseline to 53% at six years and nocturia from 20% to 38%. Radical treatment impacts were immediate and continued over six years. After RP, 95% reported ED persisting for 85% at six years, while after EBRT this was 69% an

questions.question: At twelve years in the ProtecT trial, which treatment group had the lowest rate of nocturia (voiding at least twice per night)?
questions.explanation: The text states that nocturia occurred in 34% in the prostatectomy group, compared with 48% in the radiotherapy group and 47% in the AM group at twelve years. No brachytherapy data is mentioned for nocturia.

questions.question: According to the guidelines, what should patients eligible for active surveillance be advised regarding global quality of life compared to radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy?

options.text: Global quality of life is equivalent for up to five years
Global quality of life is equivalent for up to 10 years
Global quality of life shows no difference in sexual function specifically
Global quality of life is equivalent for the patient's lifetime

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that patients eligible for active surveillance should be advised that global quality of life is equivalent for up to five years compared to radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy.
questions.section_title: 8.3.1.b. Recommendations for quality of life in men undergoing local treatments
questions.source_text: Recommendations Strength rating Advise patients eligible for active surveillance that global quality of life is equivalent for up to five years compared to radical prostatectomy or external beam radiotherapy (RT). Strong Discuss the negative impact of surgery on urinary and sexual function, as well as the negative impact of RT on bowel function with patients. Strong Advise patients treated with brachytherapy of the negative impact on irritative urinary symptomatology at one year but not after fi

questions.question: What should patients treated with brachytherapy be advised about regarding irritative urinary symptomatology?

options.text: No impact on urinary symptoms at any time point
Negative impact on irritative urinary symptomatology at one year but not after five years
Negative impact on irritative urinary symptomatology that persists indefinitely
Negative impact on bowel function at one year but not after five years

questions.explanation: The guideline states that patients treated with brachytherapy should be advised of the negative impact on irritative urinary symptomatology at one year but not after five years. Bowel function impact is associated with external beam radiotherapy, not brachytherapy.

questions.question: According to the ProtecT trial results at twelve years, which treatment group had the highest rate of faecal leakage?
questions.explanation: The text states that 'faecal leakage affected 12% in the radiotherapy group, compared with 6% in the other groups by year twelve.'

questions.question: Based on the PACE-A trial comparing prostatectomy and SBRT at 24 months, which statement is correct?

options.text: SBRT showed worse sexual function than surgery
Both groups had equal urinary bother scores with better overall sexual function in the SBRT group
Surgery had more bowel symptoms than SBRT
Both groups had identical dropout rates during the study

questions.explanation: The text states 'the authors show an equal urinary bother score, with more irritative symptoms and bowel symptoms for SBRT. The overall sexual function is better in the SBRT group.' The study also showed differential dropout with only 50% of the surgery group completing.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what were the outcomes of exercise programs during radiation therapy combined with androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?

options.text: Improved cardiovascular fitness and muscle function with reduction in urinary toxicity
Improved sexual function and reduction in cancer worry
Improved quality of life and cure rate of approximately 60%
Reduced depression and improved relationship satisfaction

questions.explanation: The text states that exercise programs during RT combined with ADT result in consistent benefits for cardiovascular fitness (SMD 0.83) and muscle function (SMD 1.30) with a reduction in urinary toxicity (SMD -0.71). The other options describe outcomes from nurse-led rehabilitation or surgical interventions, not exercise programs.
questions.source_text: In men with localised disease, nurse-led multidisciplinary rehabilitation (addressing sexual functioning, cancer worry, relationship issues, depression, and managing bowel and urinary function problems) provided positive short-term effects (four months) on sexual function (effect size 0.45) and long-term (twelve months) positive effects on sexual limitation (effect size 0.5) and cancer worry (effect size 0.51) [1614]. Exercise programs during RT combined with ADT result in consistent benefits fo

questions.question: What did the multicenter double-blind RCT (n=423) of tadalafil in men with normal pretreatment erectile function undergoing nerve-sparing radical prostatectomy demonstrate?

options.text: Daily tadalafil 5mg improved EPIC sexual domain scores compared to on-demand use or placebo
On-demand tadalafil 20mg was superior to daily dosing
No difference was found between any dosing regimens
Daily tadalafil 5mg improved urinary continence outcomes

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that in the multicenter double-blind RCT, tadalafil 5mg once per day improved participants' EPIC sexual domain scores (least-squares mean difference +9.6) compared to 20mg on-demand or placebo at nine months. The other options are not mentioned in the text regarding this study.
questions.source_text: In men with localised disease, nurse-led multidisciplinary rehabilitation (addressing sexual functioning, cancer worry, relationship issues, depression, and managing bowel and urinary function problems) provided positive short-term effects (four months) on sexual function (effect size 0.45) and long-term (twelve months) positive effects on sexual limitation (effect size 0.5) and cancer worry (effect size 0.51) [1614]. Exercise programs during RT combined with ADT result in consistent benefits fo

questions.question: In men with T1-T3 disease undergoing RT and ADT, what did the combined nurse-led psychological support and physiotherapist-led multidisciplinary rehabilitation programme achieve at three-year follow-up?

options.text: Significant improvement in all quality of life domains
Fewer moderate-to-severe bowel problems compared to control group
Sustained urinary function improvements in all participants
Reduced need for continued androgen deprivation therapy

questions.explanation: The text states that at three-year follow-up, fewer participants had moderate-to-severe bowel problems in the intervention group (3%) versus the control group (14%, p=0.016). The text also specifies that urinary function benefits were only maintained in those with moderate-severe urinary problems at baseline, not all participants.
questions.source_text: Similar to men treated with a radical approach, in men with T1-T3 disease undergoing RT and ADT, a combined nurse-led psychological support and physiotherapist-led multidisciplinary rehabilitation programme has reported improvements in QoL. Specifically, this intervention involved action planning concerning patients’ needs related to lifestyle changes, weight control, toilet habits, sexuality and psychological problems. This was complemented with pelvic floor muscle therapy. Improvements in urin

questions.question: According to the meta-analysis including 34 studies and 2,741 men, what did supervised exercise significantly improve in men on ADT?

options.text: Prostate-specific antigen levels and tumor markers
Fatigue, cancer-specific QoL, and muscular strength
Serum testosterone levels and bone mineral density
Cardiovascular parameters and lipid profiles only

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the meta-analysis including 34 studies and 2,741 men confirmed supervised exercise significantly improves fatigue (SMD -0.32), cancer-specific QoL (SMD 0.24), and muscular strength, with the greatest effects seen in combined aerobic and resistance training.
questions.source_text: Similar to men treated with a radical approach, in men with T1-T3 disease undergoing RT and ADT, a combined nurse-led psychological support and physiotherapist-led multidisciplinary rehabilitation programme has reported improvements in QoL. Specifically, this intervention involved action planning concerning patients’ needs related to lifestyle changes, weight control, toilet habits, sexuality and psychological problems. This was complemented with pelvic floor muscle therapy. Improvements in urin

questions.question: According to the text, which of the following side effects most strongly correlates with regret after prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'Urinary incontinence most strongly correlates with regret after prostatectomy.'
questions.source_text: Several treatments with curative intent for localised PCa are available all with comparable ten-year OS [612]. The treatments vary in terms of the incidence of major side effects, including urinary symptoms, bowel symptoms and compromised sexual functioning [1510,1511,1652]. For this reason, patients’ treatment preferences, in which they weigh expected benefits against likely side effects, are a central consideration in shared decision-making and in making informed treatment decisions [1653-1655

questions.question: What does the text state regarding the relationship between positive surgical margins and prostate cancer-related mortality?

options.text: Positive surgical margins are a strong predictor of increased PC-related mortality
Positive surgical margins directly correlate with higher PC-related mortality
Positive surgical margins are not directly associated with increased PC-related mortality
Positive surgical margins double the risk of PC-related mortality

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'Interestingly, positive surgical margins are not directly associated with an increased risk of PC-related mortality.'

questions.question: According to the guideline text, what was the association between autonomous active decision-making by patients and outcomes after prostatectomy?

options.text: Autonomous decision-making was associated with less regret regardless of the surgical method chosen
Autonomous decision-making led to better oncological outcomes
Autonomous decision-making was associated with more postoperative complications
Autonomous decision-making had no significant impact on patient satisfaction

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'Autonomous active decision-making by patients was associated with less regret after prostatectomy regardless of the method chosen.'
questions.source_text: Shared decision-making can increase patients’ comfort when confronted with management decisions but has been shown to improve health outcome [1665] and additional training appears to be needed for healthcare professionals guiding patients [1666]. Patient education decreased PSA testing [1667] and increased adherence to AS protocols [1668,1669]. questions.question: According to the guideline, what are the two essential components that a decision aid should incorporate to optimise efficacy?

options.text: Educate as well as promote shared decision-making
Provide detailed clinical data and statistical analysis
Offer multiple treatment alternatives and their costs

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states: 'A decision aid should educate as well as promote shared decision-making to optimise efficacy.'

questions.question: What is the recommended duration of supervised combined aerobic and resistance exercise for males on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
questions.explanation: The guideline strongly recommends offering males on ADT 12 weeks of supervised combined aerobic and resistance exercise by trained exercise specialists.
questions.section_title: 8.3.2.e. Recommendations for quality of life in men undergoing systemic treatments
questions.source_text: Recommendations Strength rating Offer males on androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) 12 weeks of supervised (by trained exercise specialists) combined aerobic and resistance exercise. Strong Advise males on ADT to maintain a healthy weight and diet, stop smoking, reduce alcohol to ≤ 2 units daily, and have yearly screening for diabetes and hypercholesterolemia. Ensure that calcium and vitamin D meet recommended levels. Strong Offer males after any radical treatment specialist nurse-led, m

questions.question: According to the guidelines, when should anti-resorptive therapy be offered to males on long-term ADT?

options.text: Only when bone mineral density T-score is below -2.5
Only when annual bone loss exceeds 5%
When BMD T-score < -2.5 OR with additional clinical risk factor for fracture OR when annual bone loss exceeds 5%
Only when there is an additional clinical risk factor for fracture

questions.explanation: The guideline states anti-resorptive therapy should be offered when ANY of these conditions are met: BMD T-score < -2.5, OR an additional clinical risk factor for fracture exists, OR when annual bone loss on ADT exceeds 5%.
